mechanism: Advertising exposure triggers competitive threat perception and physiological arousal (testosterone elevation), which activates behavioral aggression responses. The mechanism combines social perception of other consumers as competitive obstacles with hormonal preparation for physical confrontation.

Color saturation variation affects visual attention capture through arousal induction, which in turn influences perceived size estimates. A three-stage causal pathway: saturation increases arousal, which increases attention, which increases size perception.
domain: visual perception, color psychology, consumer cognition

mechanism: Bimodal rating distributions trigger inferences about product polarization and self-expressiveness. Consumers with low self-concept clarity integrate these inferences to increase preference for polarizing products as vehicles for identity signaling and self-definition.

Latent variable measurement and structural prediction: current money management stress and expected future financial security are estimated via survey items; mediation analysis and moderation by income level test effects on overall well-being, controlling for objective financial variables.
domain: financial well-being, subjective assessment, life satisfaction

mechanism: Self-construal (independent vs. interdependent) and group size jointly determine attention allocation to others' preferences. Differential attention to others mediates how consumers balance self and others' preferences in joint consumption choices via modulation of preference weighting.

Self-concept clarity acts as a structural moderator: quitting an identity-relevant subscription signals identity change and threatens self-concept stability, so low-clarity consumers retain subscriptions more than high-clarity consumers; effect is identity-relevant but not neutral subscriptions.
domain: subscription economics, identity theory, consumer retention
